By Shawn Twing
Why Measure Results?
Let’s start with an obvious question — why do we measure
results?
It’s not for the fancy charts and graphs. My view is that we
measure results for two reasons — to understand reality
objectively, and then to take informed action based on that
understanding to make improvements.
If you accept this view of reporting, then every metric we
report needs to give us objective information that we can use
to make improvements. Everything else may be fascinating, but
it only matters if it’s:
�. objectively true and,
�. useful.
Let’s start with a few assumptions.
I’m imagining that you have a multi-page presell site that’s four or
more pages long with a meaningful, trackable call to action on the
last page (e.g., an opt in or purchase).
Measuring Results
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 25 --

And that you’re sending traf�c to that MPPS with a paid Google
or Facebook ad (or both). The very basic structure would look
something like this:
Google/Facebook Ad > MPPS Page 1 (content) > Page 2 (content) > Page 3
(content) > Page 4 (Optin / Purchase)
Next, you’ll need to de�ne what is effective reporting for YOU.
Start with deciding on what you want to achieve with your ads —
your goal?
Is it to get someone to opt-in? To purchase? To raise their hand as
having a certain problem?
E�ective reporting is re�ective of intent.
I can’t tell you exactly what matters for your speci�c MPPS
without knowing exactly what you’re trying to accomplish.
However, I can explain some ways to think about reporting and
identify a few things everyone should track to kickstart your
thinking.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 25 --

What to Measure & What Not to
Measure
The ultimate goal we care about most is that �nal step; opt-in or
purchase.
That’s how we judge success quantitatively (I’m going to assume
that the fact you’re using an SOI-informed approach has improved
the quality of those leads/purchases.
However, it’s still critically important to track customer lifetime
revenue by segment for the most accurate understanding of
performance.)
In a perfect world, every visitor who clicked on an ad would also
opt-in or make a purchase. That is highly unlikely, but that’s what
perfect would look like.
Click ad > consume all content > opt-in > buy every o�er.
That’s a clear, objective standard for perfect success (and that’s
what we’re always measuring against).
One signi�cant problem with reporting and metrics is that we
have so much data available we want to see it all.
Most data we have access to is TBU — true, but useless — or it’s
supporting data that answers a question we haven’t asked (and
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 25 --

aren’t ready to act on).
A principle I live and work by is to simplify, and then get really
good at the simple.
Here’s how I think about metrics, and how I use them effectively
to produce results.
The �rst distinction we have to make is recognizing that all
metrics are not equally valuable. In broad terms, there are two
types of metrics that matter — performance metrics and
management metrics.
Management Metrics
Reporting gets fuzzy when we blend management and
performance metrics.
I see this happen a lot. The overall goal is X (for example,
acquiring customers at break even), and a business owner or
management team asks about underlying factors like cost per
click.
Don’t fall into this trap.
We want objective outcomes that de�ne success (management
metrics) and objective measurements of those outcomes
(performance metrics).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 25 --

If there are problems to �x or possibilities for improvement, we
can then (and only then) turn to management metrics which are
relative to ad platforms, strategies, etc.
Several years ago I had the privilege to spend a half day with Peter
Schutz, the �rst American-born CEO of Porsche.
One of the most memorable lessons he shared with the small
business group I belonged to at the time was a critical choice
managers need to make when working with others.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 25 --

Managers (and owners) need to specify the outcomes they
want to achieve, or the methods they want someone to use.
Here’s what I want you to accomplish, OR here is what I want you
to do. The distinction he made is that a manager/owner can’t do
both; here’s what I want you to accomplish and here’s how you
need to do it.
There’s a profound lesson in that wisdom for anyone who works
with other people and that lesson applies to this distinction
between performance metrics (outcomes) and management
metrics (methods).
If you’re in a position as a business owner or executive to manage
a person, team, or agency, make this choice wisely.
My recommendation is to manage by (and concentrate on)
performance metrics and let the people who are doing the work
focus on the management metrics that are driving those
results.
We have a rule in my business — the person closest to the work
makes the decision.
That means, for example, that I am responsible for
identifying strategic outcomes while someone on my team is
responsible for making the choices that will then support
those outcomes.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 25 --

Performance Metrics
Performance metrics describe how something performed in a
meaningful way (for example, the cost to acquire a lead or a
customer).
These usually answer the good/bad, better/worse questions I hear
most often from leadership teams and business owners.
Are my paid ads performing well?
Are my paid ads performing better or worse relative to the same
period of time last year, last month, last week?
Good, bad, better, worse all refer to the intended outcome of the MPPS.
If the goal is to acquire customers at break even and you’re
spending $100 to earn $100 in revenue, then it’s easy to say
objectively that your MPPS is performing well.
If that same MPPS generated $200 in revenue for $50 in ad spend
during the same time last year, it’s objectively accurate to
conclude that the MPPS is doing well relative to its overall goal,
and signi�cantly worse than it performed last year relative to that
same goal.
Clear, concise, objective.
That’s what we want.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 25 --

Let’s talk about some speci�c metrics I like to use and why I
believe they’re meaningful and actionable.
Performance metrics tell me how my paid ads + MPPS are
performing as a whole, systemically.
For example:
▪ Paid ad conversion rate (the number of people who opt in / purchase
divided by the total number of people who click on an ad). Let’s
assume that 100 people click on an ad and ten opt-in — that’s a 10%
conversion rate.
▪ Cost to acquire a lead / customer (total ad spend divided by total
leads/purchases). Using the same example as above — 100 people, 10
leads, and an ad spend of $100, the cost per lead would be $10.
Management metrics tell me how the parts of my MPPS that I
can affect are performing.
There are dozens of metrics we could use for Facebook and
AdWords.
That level of detail here won’t be as helpful to guide your thinking
(and it can be found elsewhere). For purposes of this discussion,
let’s focus on the metrics I use to measure MPPS performance and
�nd opportunities for improvement.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 25 --

First-to-Second Page Conversions
The most important metric — mentioned in a previous lesson — is
�rst to second page conversion rate. This is the number of
people who see my second page relative to the number who see
my �rst page.
For example, if I paid for 100 people to see the �rst page in my
MPPS and 50 clicked through to page two, my �rst to second page
conversion rate would be 50%.
In my opinion, this is the most important management metric
for your MPPS.
It’s a very powerful way to assess market to message match, to
compare audiences objectively, to determine the strength of our
copy, and much more.
Let’s imagine a scenario.
We’re targeting a very narrow POP that includes men and women.
Our audience targeting is exactly the same for both genders but
we’re targeting men and women separately in our traf�c
campaigns.
Audience #1 is men, audience #2 is women.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 25 --

After 100 visitors from each audience have seen page one of our
landing page we notice that audience #1 (men) has a �rst to
second page conversion rate of 50%, and audience #2 (women) has
a �rst to second page conversion rate of 20%.
Is this useful to know.
Absolutely.
Can we act on it?
Absolutely.
In this example the �rst thing I would do is pause ad spend to
women and allocate all ad spend to the male audience. Then I would
develop a hypothesis about why performance was so di�erent, and
then act on that hypothesis and re-test.
I wouldn’t hold the high-performing audience hostage to that testing
because it has already performed well. Testing would focus on an
alternate version, visible only to audience #2.
That simple distinction would have taught you two things — �rst,
gender matters in your POP, and second, you can spend money
e�ectively on audiences that are performing while continuing to test /
re-test audiences that are not until those perform well too.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 25 --

Second-to-Third, Third-to-Fourth,
etc. Page Conversions
After �rst to second page conversion you won’t be surprised to
know that second to third, third to fourth, etc. are the next metrics
I recommend.
My primary concern is the overall FLOW of visitors through a
MPPS, and my focus is prioritized by relative value (which means
I identify the most impactful bottlenecks and act on those).
Let’s look at a hypothetical example:
▪ Page one: 1,000 visitors
▪ Page two: 500 visitors (50% page one to page two conversion rate)
▪ Page three: 400 visitors (80% page two to page three conversion
rate)
▪ Page four: 200 visitors (50% page three to page four conversion
rate)
▪ Opt-ins on page four: 50 (25% opt in rate for that page)
Where’s the most impactful lever we can pull?
Before we answer that question, let’s convert the numbers
above to dollars. I �nd that clari�es thinking must faster. We’ll
repeat the same example, but let’s assume it costs us $1 to get a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 25 --

visitor to page one.
Here’s what the numbers look like:
▪ Page one: 1,000 visitors = $1/visitor
▪ Page two: 500 visitors = $2/visitor
▪ Page three: 400 visitors = $2.5/visitor
▪ Page four: 200 visitors = $5/visitor
▪ Opt-ins: 50 = $20/opt-in
Where are the highest-leverage opportunities to improve this
MPPS assuming $1/click is constant?
Remember, “improvement” means it costs less to acquire an
opt-in because that’s the goal of this MPPS.
Let’s look at four different scenarios — each one improving a
single metric by 10% overall. I want to go through this scenario-by-
scenario for two reasons:
▪ First, to show you the actual data.
▪ And second, to show you how to approach this exercise for yourself
to determine where your energy and attention is best spent.
Scenario #1
Improve page-one to page-two conversion rate from 50% to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 25 --

60% while all other numbers remain the same. This is what this
would look like with 1,000 clicks:
▪ Page one: 1,000 visitors = $1/visitor
▪ Page two: 600 visitors = $1.67/visitor
▪ Page three: 440 visitors = $2.27/visitor
▪ Page four: 240 visitors = $4.16/visitor
▪ Opt-ins: 60 = $16.67/opt-in.
Net result: a 10% improvement for page-one to page-two
conversion rates yields a 17% reduction (-$3.33) in cost per lead.
Scenario #2
Improve page-two to page-three conversion rate from 80% to
90% while all other numbers remain the same. This is what this
would look like with 1,000 clicks:
▪ Page one: 1,000 visitors = $1/visitor
▪ Page two: 500 visitors = $2/visitor
▪ Page three: 450 visitors = $2.22/visitor
▪ Page four: 225 visitors = $4.44/visitor
▪ Opt-ins: 56 = $17.86/opt-in
Net result: a 10% improvement for page-two to page-three
conversion rates yields a 11% reduction (-$2.14) in cost per lead.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 25 --

Scenario #3
Improve page-three to page-four conversion rate from 50% to
60% while all other numbers remain the same. This is what this
would look like with 1,000 clicks:
▪ Page one: 1,000 visitors = $1/visitor
▪ Page two: 500 visitors = $2/visitor
▪ Page three: 400 visitors = $2.5/visitor
▪ Page four: 240 visitors = $4.16/visitor
▪ Opt-ins: 60 = $16.67/opt-in
Net result: a 10% improvement for page three to page four
conversion rates yields a 17% reduction (-$3.33) in cost per lead.
Scenario #4
Improve opt-in rate from 25% to 35% while all other numbers
remain the same. This is what this would look like with 1,000
clicks:
▪ Page one: 1,000 visitors = $1/visitor
▪ Page two: 500 visitors = $2/visitor
▪ Page three: 400 visitors = $2.5/visitor
▪ Page four: 200 visitors = $5/visitor
▪ Opt-ins: 70 = $14.28/opt-in
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 25 --

Net result: a 10% improvement for opt-in conversion rates yields
a 29% reduction (-$5.75) in cost per lead.
The math tells us that the most impactful scenario is #4 — it’s
nearly twice as impactful as the two runners up.
There are other factors to consider, of course, like the time
involved to make an improvement and the likelihood of actually
making that improvement.
If scenario #4 required 10x the time/energy/dollars cost with a 5%
likelihood of success we might have to reconsider.
But, let’s not get lost in the details.
The math is the math and, all things being equal it tells us exactly
where to focus our energy and attention to make the most
signi�cant improvement.
This is why accurate, objective reporting matters. The way we’re
using it here informs and guides effective decision making.
These three scenarios also show the way to create exponential
gains over time. We’ll talk about that in more detail in the next
lesson — for now, let’s look at a simple example.
Let’s assume we were able to get a 10% improvement in each of
these key metrics simultaneously — here’s what that would look
like:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 25 --

▪ Page one: 1,000 visitors = $1/visitor
▪ Page two: 600 visitors = $1.67/visitor
▪ Page three: 540 visitors = $1.85/visitor
▪ Page four: 324 visitors = $3.09/visitor
▪ Opt-ins: 113 = $8.85/opt-in.
That’s the power of compounding incremental gains.
Will those gains happen overnight?
Probably not. But knowing the metrics that matter and then
taking action on those metrics in priority order based on what is
most impactful to the overall intent of your MPPS is the path to
long-term success with paid traf�c.
To summarize before we move on, if you’re the CEO, chief janitor,
and paid advertising specialist for your business, I still encourage
you to look at those two levels separately.
Understand the story that the performance metrics are telling you
— something is “good” or “bad” relative to the goal you set, and
that something is better/worse/the same relative to a similar
period of time in the past.
Once you’ve set that frame, then use management metrics to
drive positive change in overall performance.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 25 --

It’s possible, for example, that high cost per click is a causal factor
in your high cost-per-lead — if that’s something you can affect,
then do it.
But only make that decision after you’ve worked your way down
from the top-level performance metrics to the supporting
management metrics.
Report — and Act On — Reality
One of my favorite authors and thinkers, Robert Fritz, makes the
comment that “reality is an acquired taste.”
In my experience, that is an understatement.
The goal of reporting is to communicate objective reality as
clearly and concisely as possible so that the data can be acted
upon. Fuzzy numbers, incomplete data, unsupported assumptions,
etc. all cloud our view.
Here’s a favorite example from my own industry.
Many marketing agencies don’t include their fees in reporting
calculations.
Seriously.
They’re described as a cost of doing business (much like rent and
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 25 --

electricity I suppose).
Let’s think about how that would affect our metrics (if you’re
using an agency or subcontractor to manage your paid ad spend).
If you’re doing the work yourself, don’t skip this section. There’s
something at the end for you to think about too.
Let’s assume you’re spending $5,000/month on Facebook and
Google AdWords ad spend combined, and you’re paying an
agency $1,000/month to manage that ad spend.
To make my math easier and my point more concise, let’s assume
that $5,000 is spent on a single, perfect audience (bravo, BTW, for
keeping your efforts so focused).
Now let’s assume that the goal of this MPPS is to generate
customers at break even. To do that we have an opt-in and
inexpensive offer by email within three days of opt-in.
Now, let’s look at some numbers.
Version #1 — $5,000 in ad spend generates $5,000 in sales.
Yahoo! We did it (everyone pats each other on the back). What
about the $1,000 paid to the agency? That’s just a cost of doing
business…
This is magical thinking — as in Jack and the Beanstalk-type
magic. As an agency owner I prefer to get paid with real money I
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 25 --

can spend (!) — which means the person/company paying me that
money could have saved or spent it on something else too. It’s real
money, a real expense, and 100% related to the activities required
to create that $5,000 in sales which means it needs to be part of
the calculation.
Version #2 — $5,000 in ad spend + $1,000 in management fees
generates $5,000 in sales. $6,000 to generate $5,000 is not break
even.
Simple. Honest. Objective.
Does that mean the agency’s role was “bad” or “good”?
No. It simply means that the total costs to generate $5,000 in
revenue were $6,000, and that’s -$1,000 relative to the stated goal.
That is objective reality.
This gets complicated when agencies and subcontractors have
more than one role.
Many agencies (including my own) setup tracking, complete
design/build work, report performance, advise on strategy, etc.
Those activities need to be tracked separately for obvious reasons.
Any activity related to traf�c generation, however, should be
included in your ad spend calculations.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 25 --

Do what makes sense for your business. As long as it’s
intellectually defensible, don’t sweat the details.
What about agencies that charge based on a percentage of ad
spend instead? Don’t get me started on that.
Incentives drive behavior. Period.
What if you’re a business owner/partner who is responsible for
managing paid traf�c?
To avoid distorting your metrics I suggest that you track your
time, assign a monetary value to that time, and include those
numbers in your calculation.
That value can be an honest appraisal of your current hourly
income, or an equally honest appraisal of your “sweat equity” if
you’re not generating any income yet.
Other alternatives include researching costs in your market for
this work and calculating your time with those rates.
Why is this important?
Because our goal is understanding reality objectively. If you spend
25 hours per week managing paid traf�c campaigns and
$1,000/week on ad spend to generate $1,000 in sales, did you really
break even?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 25 --

Only if you’re willing to work for free for 25 hours per week! In
that same scenario, if you decide to of�oad that 25 hours per week
to an employee or subcontractor @ $50 per hour, that’s $2,250 to
generate $1,000.
That doesn’t seem like break even to me.
Reporting Tools
Before we wrap this section up let’s talk about the software and
tools you’ll need to calculate the metrics I have identi�ed in
this lesson.
My recommendation is a (free) Google Analytics account for
tracking, a spreadsheet for analysis, and a piece of paper or
index cards for data capture.
Paper? Yes, paper.
The more sophisticated the dashboards we create the less involved
we are in understanding the data that informs those dashboards.
I want you to really understand at a deep, visceral level what your
data is telling you. Listen to the story it’s telling and then act on
what you’ve learned.
In Smarter, Better, Faster Charles Duhigg described the way in
which elementary school teachers transformed their slick
www.getwsodo.com

-- 21 of 25 --

electronic dashboards into real, insightful data (which produced
real, impactful results in their schools).
Physically engaging with the data — writing it down on paper,
using color-coded index cards, and other tactics made the data
harder to process at �rst:
“Rather than simply receiving information, teachers were forced to
engage with it. The [Elementary Initiative] had worked because instead
of passively absorbing data, teachers made it ‘dis�uent’—harder to
process at �rst, but stickier once it was really understood.
By scribbling out statistics and testing preconceptions, teachers had
�gured out how to use all the information they were receiving.
The Elementary Initiative, paradoxically, had made data more
cumbersome to absorb—but more useful. And from those index cards
and hand-drawn graphs, better classrooms emerged,” Duhigg wrote.
I encourage you to do the same thing.
Track your data with Google Analytics.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate your various conversion rates.
(You can use GA’s funnel visualization tools to make this easier, but don’t
just accept the data, force yourself to engage with it even if that means
copying those numbers into a spreadsheet.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 22 of 25 --

And then manually add in anything else that’s relevant. Ad spend.
Opt-in rate. Purchase rate. Purchase amount.
Is this a lot of work?
Yes, at �rst it is. That’s the point.
We don’t want slick pie charts we ignore — we want information
we can act on. In my experience, the best way to understand and
internalize that information is working to get it, and then
working to make sense of it.
Questions will arise — write them down!
Once you’ve identi�ed some questions, go look for answers. If you
can’t �nd answers, identify and test a hypothesis and then capture
and quantify your results.
If you do this once a week for a year you will compound 50+
improvements.
Think about that for a moment.
Reporting Rhythm
The frequency with which you look at data changes over time and
depending on what you’re looking for.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 23 of 25 --

At �rst, if you’re getting a signi�cant volume of traf�c (100+
visitors per day per segment) I recommend looking at data daily.
Pay attention to the ways in which the day of the week affects
performance.
In my experience there’s often a dramatic difference in
performance Monday — Thursday vs. Friday — Sunday.
Once you’ve accumulated enough data, start looking at weekly
trends. That’s a different perspective that’ll give you different
insights. Then monthly, quarterly or seasonally depending on
your particular business.
The point isn’t the increments, it’s the different vantage points to
see different trends.
As long as those increments are meaningful to your business then
use that vantage point. Relative performance is important too.
▪ How did today compare to yesterday?
▪ This week vs. last week?
▪ The �rst week of the month relative to the �rst week of the previous
month?
▪ What’s the year to date trend vs. this week’s performance?
Pay attention to the factors that in�uence your results, eliminate
those factors that have negative in�uences, and support those
www.getwsodo.com

-- 24 of 25 --

factors that have positive in�uences. Add the compounding
element of time and you’re on your way to exponential
improvements.
More on that coming up next.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 25 of 25 --
